sections.title: Comparative Forms
sections.explanation: Greek has two ways to form comparatives. The most common is 'πιο + adjective + από' (more...than): 'πιο μεγάλος από' (bigger than), 'πιο ακριβός από' (more expensive than). The word 'από' introduces the second element of the comparison. Some adjectives also have a synthetic comparative with the suffix -τερος/-τερη/-τερο: μεγαλύτερος (bigger), μικρότερος (smaller), ακριβότερος (more expensive). Several common adjectives have irregular comparatives: καλύτερος (better, from καλός), χειρότερος (worse, from κακός). To say 'as...as' use the construction 'τόσο...όσο': Είναι τόσο μεγάλος όσο εγώ (He is as tall as me). Both comparative strategies are correct and widely used in everyday Greek.
sections.tip: Use 'πιο + adjective + από' as a safe default for any adjective. The -τερος forms are more formal and only exist for common adjectives.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'more expensive than' using the πιο construction?
exercises.explanation: 'πιο ακριβός από' is the standard comparative construction: πιο + adjective + από. 'ακριβότατος' is a superlative and 'τόσο ακριβός' is incomplete without 'όσο'.

exercises.prompt: Which word is the irregular comparative of 'καλός' (good)?
exercises.explanation: 'καλύτερος' is the irregular synthetic comparative of καλός. While 'πιο καλός' is also used informally, 'καλύτερος' is the standard form. 'καλότερος' does not exist.
'τόσο...όσο' expresses equality: 'τόσο μεγάλος όσο' (as big as). 'πιο...από' means 'more...than' and 'ο/η/το πιο' introduces a superlative.

sections.title: Superlative Forms
sections.explanation: Greek has two types of superlatives. The relative superlative (the most...) is formed with the definite article + πιο + adjective: 'ο πιο μεγάλος' (the biggest, masculine), 'η πιο μεγάλη' (the biggest, feminine), 'το πιο μεγάλο' (the biggest, neuter). The article must agree in gender with the noun. Alternatively, use the article + synthetic comparative: 'ο μεγαλύτερος'. The absolute superlative (very, extremely) expresses the highest degree without comparison. It is formed either with 'πολύ' + adjective: 'πολύ μεγάλος' (very big), or with the suffix -τατος/-τατη/-τατο added to the stem: 'μεγαλύτατος' (very/most big, formal/literary). The -τατος form is mostly found in formal writing; 'πολύ' is standard in speech.
sections.tip: For the relative superlative, match the article gender to the noun: ο πιο καλός δάσκαλος, η πιο καλή δασκάλα, το πιο καλό παιδί.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the best' referring to a masculine noun (ο δάσκαλος)?
exercises.explanation: For a masculine noun use the masculine article ο: 'ο πιο καλός' or 'ο καλύτερος'. 'το πιο καλό' uses the neuter article and 'πολύ καλός' is an absolute superlative meaning 'very good', not 'the best'.
'πολύ ακριβός' means very expensive — the absolute superlative using πολύ. 'The most expensive' would be 'ο πιο ακριβός' and 'more expensive than' would be 'πιο ακριβός από'.

exercises.prompt: Which suffix marks the formal absolute superlative in Greek?
exercises.explanation: The suffix -τατος/-τατη/-τατο marks the formal/literary absolute superlative (e.g., μεγαλύτατος). The suffix -τερος forms the comparative, not the superlative.

sections.title: Comparison Sentences
sections.explanation: Let's put comparatives and superlatives to work in full sentences. When comparing two things, the structure is: Subject + verb + comparative adjective + από + second element. Note that adjectives agree in gender with the subject noun: 'Η Αθήνα είναι μεγαλύτερη από τη Θεσσαλονίκη' — μεγαλύτερη is feminine to match Αθήνα (η). After 'από', nouns appear in the accusative case: 'από τη Θεσσαλονίκη', 'από τον χειμώνα'. Key irregular comparatives to memorize: καλός → καλύτερος (good → better), κακός → χειρότερος (bad → worse), πολύς → περισσότερος (much/many → more), λίγος → λιγότερος (few/little → fewer/less). These irregular forms follow the same gender/number/case agreement rules as regular adjectives.
sections.tip: After 'από' in a comparison, the noun takes the accusative case. Remember: 'από τον' (masculine), 'από τη(ν)' (feminine), 'από το' (neuter).

examples.el: Η Αθήνα είναι μεγαλύτερη από τη Θεσσαλονίκη.
Το καλοκαίρι είναι πιο ζεστό από τον χειμώνα.
Αυτό το εστιατόριο είναι καλύτερο από εκείνο.
examples.en: This restaurant is better than that one.

examples.el: Ο καφές είναι χειρότερος από τον χυμό.
examples.en: The coffee is worse than the juice.
I have less money than you. (λίγος → λιγότερος)

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence: 'Η Ελλάδα είναι ___ από την Κύπρο.' (bigger)
exercises.explanation: 'Ελλάδα' is feminine (η), so the comparative adjective must be feminine: μεγαλύτερη. 'μεγαλύτερος' is masculine and 'πιο μεγάλο' is neuter — neither matches.
'πολύς' has the irregular comparative 'περισσότερος' (more). 'πολύτερος' does not exist. 'χειρότερος' is the comparative of κακός (bad → worse).

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'The train is faster than the bus'?
options: Το τρένο είναι πιο γρήγορο από λεωφορείο.
Το τρένο είναι γρηγορύτερο από λεωφορείου.
Το τρένο είναι πιο γρήγορο από το λεωφορείο.
exercises.explanation: The correct sentence uses 'από το λεωφορείο' (accusative with the article). Dropping the article after 'από' is incorrect for a specific noun, and 'γρηγορύτερο' is not a standard form — use 'πιο γρήγορο' or 'γρηγορότερο'.
